author: @stevenw4549
text: Nice job as always. Your going to have a small museum there of various mechanisms. Drawings aren't as good as seeing the actual devise. I was wondering if you have an e-mail address or something? I have a few drawings of ideas you might like. I promise not to overwhelm you with e-mails LOL. By the way there are a lot of self running magnet powered motors and perpetual motion machines on YouTube. I have built some of them and so far none of them work. The one problem seems to be equilibrium. Whether it's magnets or gravity they always reach an equilibrium and they stop. Some of the ideas look convincing and logical and could solve the energy problem but it's just too bad they don't work. Have a look at some of them they can be interesting. I waisted a considerable amount of time and effort on some of them including machining and casting gears but not likely to pursue them any further. They did however satisfy my curiosity.

replies.author: @stevenw4549
replies.text: Desmond Bagley Yes it's not really a bad thing it's just a part of our learning of what is possible and what is not. I learned a few things from them myself. Also some things impossible now may not be later as something changes the equation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I keep the models mate - I like the look of them for sure. My email is robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com if it helps - cheers

author: @robgood9119
text: Completely unrelated. Have you ever thought of the possibility of recharging a battery using magnets? Would it be possible to pull possitive and negative charges to the appropriate sides using magnetic fields and mechanical movement?

replies.author: @alanclarke4646
replies.text: No. It doesn't work that way. Magnetism is caused by the SPIN of electrons. Your idea would pull electrons that spin in one direction to the positive end of the battery, and electrons spinning in the opposite direction would be pulled to the other end. As all electrons are negatively charged, the magnets would not charge the battery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well it really depends what you mean mate - after all a generator is nothing more than magnets being mechanically moved in a coil of wire - so in a sense yes. But like I say it all really depends on what you actually mean

author: @herbetone
text:
Many moons ago, well around 45 years ago one of the machines I used in my apprenticeship was a shaping machine, I took all that wonderful way it worked mechanically for granted back then.
I just found a second hand one going on Ebay going for £3.000 and when our factory closed down all our machinery went for scrap, what a waste.
 Great little video and a great demonstration.
